section.title: The Lamb and the Harvest of the Earth
section.sectionRationale: Chapter 14 directly answers chapter 13. Where the beast marked and coerced worship from those who dwell on the earth, the Lamb stands with 144,000 marked with His own name and His Father's; where the beast demanded false worship through deceptive signs, three flying angels proclaim the true, eternal gospel, announce Babylon's fall, and warn of the wrath awaiting the beast's worshipers; and the chapter closes with two harvest scenes, grain gathered and grapes trodden, that resolve the whole conflict into ingathering and judgment.
section.thesis: Against the beast's mark and forced worship, the Lamb keeps His own people, marked with His name and His Father's; three angels proclaim the eternal gospel, Babylon's fall, and the certainty of wrath for the beast's worshipers; and the chapter ends with the earth's two harvests, grain for the redeemed and grapes for the wicked, trodden in the great winepress of God's wrath.
section.frameNote: Five beats: the Lamb and the 144,000 on Mount Zion; the three angels proclaiming the gospel, Babylon's fall, and the warning of wrath; the beatitude on those who die in the Lord; the harvest of grain; and the harvest of grapes trodden in the winepress of wrath.

units.label: The Lamb and the 144,000 on Zion
units.note: the redeemed, marked with the Lamb's name

verses.text: Then I looked and saw the Lamb standing on Mount Zion, and with Him 144,000 who had His name and His Father's name written on their foreheads.
And I heard a sound from heaven like the roar of many waters and the loud rumbling of thunder. And the sound I heard was like harpists strumming their harps.
And they sang a new song before the throne and before the four living creatures and the elders. And no one could learn the song except the 144,000 who had been redeemed from the earth.

These are the ones who have not been defiled with women, for they are virgins. They follow the Lamb wherever He goes. They have been redeemed from among men as {{aparche|firstfruits}} to God and to the Lamb.
And no lie was found in their mouths; they are blameless.

units.label: Three angels: gospel, Babylon's fall, and the warning of wrath
units.note: a last call before the harvest

verses.text: Then I saw another angel flying overhead, with the eternal {{euangelion|gospel}} to proclaim to those who dwell on the earth, to every nation and tribe and tongue and people.
And he said in a loud voice, “Fear God and give Him glory, because the hour of His judgment has come. Worship the One who made the heavens and the earth and the sea and the springs of waters.”
Then a second angel followed, saying, “Fallen, fallen is Babylon the great, who has made all the nations drink the wine of the {{thymos|passion}} of her immorality.”
And a third angel followed them, calling out in a loud voice, “If anyone worships the beast and its image, and receives its mark on his forehead or on his hand,
he too will drink the wine of God's anger, poured undiluted into the cup of His {{orge|wrath}}. And he will be tormented in fire and sulfur in the presence of the holy angels and of the Lamb.
And the smoke of their torment rises forever and ever. Day and night there is no rest for those who worship the beast and its image, or for anyone who receives the mark of its name.”
Here is a call for the perseverance of the saints who keep the commandments of God and the faith of Jesus.

units.label: Blessed are the dead in the Lord

verses.text: And I heard a voice from heaven telling me to write, “Blessed are the dead, those who die in the Lord from this moment on.” “Yes,” says the Spirit, “they will rest from their labors, for their deeds will follow them.”

units.label: The harvest of grain
units.note: the Son of Man swings the sickle

verses.text: And I looked and saw a white cloud, and seated on the cloud was One like the Son of Man, with a golden crown on His head and a sharp sickle in His hand.

Then another angel came out of the temple, crying out in a loud voice to the One seated on the cloud, “Swing Your sickle and {{therizo|reap}}, because the time has come to harvest; for the crop of the earth is ripe.”
So the One seated on the cloud swung His sickle over the earth, and the earth was harvested.

units.label: The harvest of grapes and the winepress of wrath

verses.text: Then another angel came out of the temple in heaven, and he too had a sharp sickle.

Still another angel, with authority over the fire, came from the altar and called out in a loud voice to the angel with the sharp sickle, “Swing your sharp sickle and gather the clusters of grapes from the vine of the earth, because its grapes are ripe.”
So the angel swung his sickle over the earth and gathered the grapes of the earth, and he threw them into the great winepress of God's wrath.
And the winepress was trodden outside the city, and the blood that flowed from it rose as high as the bridles of the horses for a distance of 1,600 stadia.

aparche.gloss: Firstfruits, the first and consecrated portion of a larger harvest, offered to God under the law before the rest of the crop was used, Leviticus 23:10. The 144,000 are called firstfruits, a first, representative portion of a much larger redeemed harvest still to be gathered, belonging to God and to the Lamb.

euangelion.gloss: Good news, gospel. Here it is called the eternal gospel, proclaimed by an angel to every nation, tribe, tongue, and people, the same good news of God's saving purpose in Christ that stands unchanged from before the foundation of the world into the hour of final judgment.

thymos.gloss: Passion, fury, intense anger poured out. It describes both Babylon's intoxicating wine of immoral passion, which she forces the nations to drink, and God's own wine of anger, poured back on those who drink hers, a deliberate reversal in which the earth's seducer is answered in kind.

orge.gloss: Settled, righteous wrath, as distinct from a sudden outburst. It names the contents of the cup poured undiluted for those who worship the beast, God's just and unmixed judgment against persistent, willful sin, held in reserve until this hour.

therizo.gloss: To reap or harvest. The One like the Son of Man is told to swing His sickle and reap because the crop of the earth is ripe, the same verb used of gathering a field of grain, here applied to the final ingathering at the close of the age.

basic.context: This chapter directly answers chapter 13. Instead of the beast's mark, the Lamb's own people stand with Him on Mount Zion, marked with His name and His Father's. Three angels fly overhead proclaiming the eternal gospel to every nation, announcing the fall of Babylon, and warning that anyone who worships the beast will drink God's undiluted wrath. The chapter ends with two harvest scenes: grain gathered by the Son of Man, and grapes gathered and trodden in the great winepress of God's wrath.
basic.hermeneutics: Interpreters debate whether the 144,000 and their description as virgins should be read literally, as a specific, defined company, or symbolically, as a picture of the whole faithful church kept pure from spiritual adultery with Babylon. They likewise debate whether the grain harvest and grape harvest are two different outcomes, ingathering versus judgment, or one judgment scene told twice. Either way, the chapter's message is plain: the age will end in a definite, complete harvest, and no one is left ungathered.
basic.originalLanguages: The redeemed are called aparche, firstfruits, a first portion promising a larger harvest to come. An angel proclaims the euangelion, the eternal gospel, to every nation before judgment falls. Babylon's wine is called thymos, passion, and God answers it with His own thymos and orge, His settled, undiluted wrath. And the final ingathering is pictured with therizo, to reap, the ordinary word for harvesting a ripe field.
basic.typology: The firstfruits offering of the law, the first portion consecrated to God before the rest of the harvest, becomes a picture of the redeemed as the first installment of a far larger harvest still to come. The grain harvest, carried out by One like the Son of Man, echoes Jesus' own parables of a harvest at the end of the age, while the grape harvest fulfills Isaiah's picture of God alone treading the winepress of judgment.
basic.christ: The Lamb stands victorious with His marked people on Zion, and it is One like the Son of Man, Christ Himself, who is pictured swinging the sickle to gather the grain harvest, the ingathering of the redeemed. The eternal gospel proclaimed to every nation is fundamentally about Him, and even the wrath poured out is described as happening in the presence of the Lamb, showing that final judgment, no less than final salvation, belongs to Him.
basic.god: God's patience and God's justice appear together here. He sends His eternal gospel to every nation before judgment falls, giving fair warning through three angels, yet His wrath, once poured, is undiluted and complete, a cup of settled justice against a world that has traded worship of the Creator for worship of the beast and its image.
basic.crossReferences: Revelation 7:2-4 introduces the sealing of the 144,000 that this chapter assumes. Joel 3:13 supplies the picture of a ripe harvest and a full winepress. Matthew 13:24-30 and 36-43 tell of wheat and weeds gathered at the end of the age. And Isaiah 63:1-6 pictures the Lord alone treading the winepress of the nations in judgment.
basic.oneStory: Where the beast forced counterfeit worship and marked its followers for judgment, the Lamb keeps His own, marked with His true name, and sends His eternal gospel to every nation while there is still time to respond. The whole Bible moves toward this same twin ending, a harvest of ingathering for those who belong to Christ and a harvest of judgment for those who do not, both accomplished by the same Son of Man who once bore that judgment Himself.

context.literary: Chapter 14 forms the deliberate answer to chapter 13's two beasts, an interlude of hope and warning before the seven bowls. Its five scenes move from the redeemed on Zion, through angelic proclamation, to a beatitude on those who die in the Lord, and finally to two harvest visions that resolve the conflict of the beast's reign.
context.historical: Written to churches under real pressure to compromise with Rome's imperial cult and its trade networks, symbolized here as Babylon, the chapter reassures believers that visible economic and political power built on immorality and idolatry, however dominant it appears, is already fallen in God's sight and awaits certain judgment.
context.canonical: The 144,000 sealed in chapter 7 reappear here standing safely with the Lamb; the eternal gospel proclaimed to every nation echoes the Great Commission; and the two harvests anticipate both Jesus' own harvest parables and the final judgment scenes of Revelation 19 and 20.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To assure believers under pressure from the beast's counterfeit worship that the Lamb keeps and marks His own, that the gospel is still being proclaimed to every nation, and that a definite, final harvest, ingathering for the redeemed and judgment for the wicked, is certain and near.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The chapter describes the outcome of history, the ingathering of the redeemed and the judgment of the impenitent, rather than prescribing a detailed timetable. Its abiding call is to persevere in the faith of Jesus, to heed the eternal gospel while it is proclaimed, and to take seriously the reality of final judgment.

debates.question: Who are the 144,000, and what does 'not defiled with women, for they are virgins' mean?

views.label: A specific, literal company kept from a particular sin
views.case: The number and the language of virginity are read plainly as describing a defined, faithful company, possibly connected to the 144,000 sealed from the twelve tribes in chapter 7, kept from participating in the sexually immoral rites often tied to pagan idol worship and emperor cult feasts in the Roman world.

views.label: A symbolic picture of the whole redeemed church
views.case: Revelation elsewhere calls the corrupt world system 'Babylon the great' and pictures her seduction of the nations as spiritual adultery, so being undefiled 'virgins' pictures the whole church's covenant faithfulness to Christ rather than literal sexual abstinence, and 144,000, twelve squared multiplied by a thousand, is the same symbolic number of completeness used for Israel's tribes in chapter 7, now standing for the full company of the redeemed gathered with the Lamb.

debates.bearing: Either way, the company pictures those who have refused the beast's counterfeit worship and kept singular allegiance to the Lamb; the passage's force is the same, purity of devotion set against the world's spiritual adultery.

debates.question: Are the grain harvest and the grape harvest the same event told twice, or two distinct harvests?

views.label: One harvest of judgment told twice
views.case: Both scenes use a sickle and both are called a harvest, and the grape harvest explicitly ends in the winepress of wrath, so some read the grain harvest also as judgment on the unrepentant earth, the whole chapter picturing a single, final ingathering for judgment, echoing Joel 3:13.

views.label: Two harvests, one for ingathering and one for judgment
views.case: The grain harvest is carried out by One like the Son of Man Himself, wearing a golden crown, echoing Jesus' own harvest parables where grain pictures the gathering of the redeemed, Matthew 13:30 and 39, while the grape harvest is carried out by an angel with authority over fire at God's explicit command and ends in the winepress of wrath, so most readers take grain as the ingathering of the saints and grapes as judgment on the wicked.

debates.bearing: However the two scenes relate to one another, together they declare that the present age will end in a definite, complete harvest, none escape gathering, whether into Christ's kingdom or into God's judgment.

study.originalLanguages: Several loaded words carry this chapter's weight. The redeemed are called aparche, firstfruits, the first consecrated portion promising a far larger harvest to come. An angel proclaims the euangelion, the eternal gospel, to every nation before the hour of judgment arrives. Babylon's intoxicating wine is thymos, passion, and God answers her with His own thymos and orge together, His settled, undiluted wrath poured into the cup she once offered the nations. And the final ingathering, both for grain and for grapes, is described with therizo, to reap, the plain, agricultural word for a harvest whose time has fully come.

typology.type: The firstfruits offering of the law
typology.fulfillment: The 144,000 as a first, consecrated portion of a much larger redeemed harvest still to be gathered, Leviticus 23:10 and James 1:18.

typology.type: The ripe grain harvest gathered by One like the Son of Man
typology.fulfillment: Christ's own teaching of a harvest at the end of the age, separating the redeemed from the world, Matthew 13:30, 36-43.

typology.type: The grapes trodden in the winepress of wrath
typology.fulfillment: The Lord alone treading the winepress of judgment on the nations, Isaiah 63:1-6, fulfilled finally in Christ's return, Revelation 19:15.

study.christ: The Lamb stands triumphant with His marked, redeemed people on Zion even while the beast still rages on earth, and it is One like the Son of Man who is given the sickle to reap the ripe grain harvest, Christ Himself gathering His own at the close of the age. The eternal gospel proclaimed to every nation calls all people to worship the Creator before judgment falls, a proclamation that is ultimately about Him, and it is in the very presence of the Lamb that the beast's worshipers are said to be tormented, showing that final judgment, like final salvation, is entrusted to Christ.
study.god: God's patience and God's justice stand side by side. He still sends His eternal gospel to every nation, tribe, tongue, and people, giving fair and public warning through three flying angels before the harvest begins. Yet once poured, His wrath is undiluted, full strength, unmixed with mercy for those who have persistently worshiped the beast, a settled and righteous response to willful, ongoing rebellion rather than a sudden or arbitrary outburst.

crossReferences.note: The sealing of the 144,000 from the twelve tribes, the same company now seen standing safely with the Lamb on Zion.
The prophetic picture of a ripe harvest and a full winepress, background for both harvest scenes in this chapter.
Jesus' own parable of wheat and weeds gathered at the harvest, the end of the age.
The Lord alone treading the winepress of the nations in judgment, the imagery behind the grape harvest here.

study.oneStory: Where the beast branded its worshipers and coerced their allegiance, the Lamb marks His own people with His true name and calls every nation to worship the Creator while the eternal gospel is still being proclaimed. The whole biblical story moves toward this same double outcome: a harvest of ingathering for those who belong to the Lamb, pictured as firstfruits of a still larger gathering, and a harvest of judgment for those who do not, both entrusted to the very Son of Man who bore that judgment on the cross so that many could instead be gathered into His kingdom.

quiz.q: Who stands with the Lamb on Mount Zion?

options.text: The 144,000, who have His name and His Father's name written on their foreheads.
options.note: Revelation 14:1 states this directly.

options.text: An unnamed crowd bearing the mark of the beast.
options.note: This group is marked with the Lamb's and the Father's names, the opposite of the beast's mark.

options.text: Only the twelve apostles.
options.note: The number is 144,000, not twelve individuals.

options.text: No one; the Lamb stands alone.
options.note: The text explicitly says 144,000 stand with Him.

quiz.q: What does the first of the three angels proclaim?

options.text: The eternal gospel, calling every nation to fear God and worship the Creator.
options.note: Revelation 14:6-7 describes this proclamation.

options.text: The immediate destruction of the whole earth.
options.note: The message is a call to worship and fear God, not an announcement of destruction.

options.text: A message meant only for the church, not the nations.
options.note: The gospel is proclaimed to every nation, tribe, tongue, and people.

options.text: Nothing; the first angel is silent.
options.note: The first angel proclaims the eternal gospel in a loud voice.

quiz.q: What does the second angel announce?

options.text: That Babylon the great has fallen, having made the nations drink the wine of her immorality.
options.note: Revelation 14:8 gives this announcement.

options.text: That Babylon has repented and been restored.
options.note: The announcement is Babylon's fall, not her restoration.

options.text: That the beast has repented of its blasphemy.
options.note: The announcement concerns Babylon's fall, not the beast's repentance.

options.text: Nothing about Babylon at all.
options.note: Babylon's fall is precisely what the second angel proclaims.

quiz.q: What does the beatitude in verse 13 promise to those who die in the Lord?

options.text: They are blessed, will rest from their labors, and their deeds will follow them.
options.note: Revelation 14:13 states this directly.

options.text: They will be forgotten along with their labors.
options.note: The opposite is promised; their deeds follow them and they rest.

options.text: They must continue laboring in the afterlife.
options.note: They are promised rest from their labors, not continued toil.

options.text: Nothing is said about those who die in the Lord.
options.note: The verse is a specific beatitude concerning exactly this group.

quiz.q: What do the two harvest scenes at the end of the chapter depict together?

options.text: A grain harvest and a grape harvest, widely read as the gathering of the redeemed and judgment on the wicked.
options.note: Revelation 14:14-20 presents both scenes, commonly read this way.

options.text: Two identical celebrations with no connection to judgment.
options.note: The grape harvest explicitly ends in the winepress of God's wrath.

options.text: A harvest that includes only angels, with no human significance.
options.note: Both harvests concern 'the earth' and its people, not angels alone.

options.text: Nothing; the chapter ends without any harvest imagery.
options.note: The final section is built entirely around two harvest scenes.

questions: The 144,000 are called firstfruits, a first portion promising a larger harvest to come. How does knowing you are part of something bigger than yourself shape how you live for Christ today?
An angel proclaims the eternal gospel to every nation before judgment falls. Who in your life still needs to hear that same good news from you while there is time?
Verse 13 promises rest and remembrance to those who die in the Lord. How does that promise change the way you think about weariness, labor, or even your own mortality?
Babylon offered an intoxicating counterfeit of satisfaction that God's wrath finally answers. What counterfeit comforts are you tempted to drink from instead of trusting God?
The chapter ends with a certain, complete harvest, no one is left ungathered. How does the certainty of that final harvest affect the urgency and hope with which you live this week?

translationNotes.note: Firstfruits, aparche, recalls the Israelite law of consecrating the first portion of a harvest to God, Leviticus 23:10; it pictures the 144,000 as a consecrated portion of a larger harvest still to come, not the entirety of the redeemed.

Babylon the great is named here for the first time in Revelation and is developed fully in chapters 17 and 18; the wine of the passion of her immorality pictures the intoxicating, seductive nature of the world system's idolatry.

'Poured undiluted' reflects that ancient wine was normally mixed with water; describing God's wrath as undiluted pictures judgment at full strength, unmixed with any admixture of mercy for the unrepentant.

1,600 stadia is roughly 184 miles or about 296 kilometers. Some read this as a literal distance connected to the length of the land, while others read 1,600 symbolically, as 40 multiplied by 40, a number associated elsewhere with testing and judgment.

meta.notes: BSB verse text supplied pre-verified this session; no em or en dashes were present in the supplied text and none were introduced. Straight quotation marks and apostrophes were rendered as curly quotation marks to match this repo's typographic convention; no wording was altered.
